segments.s059-g1: چنان بد که is a fixed narrative phrase meaning “چنان بود که” = “so/it was such that”; here بد is a poetic/contracted form of بود (past of بودن). It introduces the clause whose subject follows (مهراب). روزی پگاه is a time expression (roughly “one morning / at dawning”), with روزی as a temporal adverb and پگاه (“dawn/early morning”) specifying the time.
segments.s059-g2: برفت and بیامد are two coordinated past verbs joined by و: برفت is an archaic/past verbal form meaning “went off/left” and بیامد means “came (back).” The prepositional phrase از آن بارگاه (“from that palace/court”) follows the verbs and is governed by از (“from”). The sequence implies he went (away) and came (back) relative to that place.
segments.s059-g3: گذر کرد is a verbal expression formed from the noun گذر (“passage/passing”) plus کرد (to do) to mean “passed” or “made a passage.” سوی is a directional preposition “toward,” and شبستان خویش is the object meaning “his bedchamber/inner room” — خوی + ش here form the possessive “خویش” (his/one’s own), with ش an enclitic marking possession.
segments.s059-g4: همی گشت is a habitual/continuous narrative phrase: همی indicates ongoing or habitual action and گشت (here intransitive) means “went about/roamed/turned.” بر گرد is a compound sense “around/about” and governs the following noun phrase بستان خویش (“his garden”), again with خوی + ش functioning as the possessive.
segments.s059-g5: دو خورشید is a metaphorical subject (“two suns,” i.e. two radiant figures). بود is the past tense copula “was/were.” اندر ایوان او is a locative phrase “in/within his iwan/portico,” with او referring back to the possessor of the iwan.
segments.s059-g6: چو here functions like “namely / as” or “being” to identify the two suns: سیندخت و رودابه is the coordinate proper‑name phrase, and ماه روی is an epithet placed after the names meaning “moon‑faced” (ماه = moon, روی = face). The adjective phrase modifies the named women.
segments.s059-g7: بیاراسته here is a descriptive participial/adjectival word meaning “adorned/arrayed” (in older orthography it may be read as بئآراسته/بِآراسته — a positive, decorated sense in context). همچو means “like/as” and introduces the comparison to باغ بهار (“a spring garden”), where باغ = garden and بهار = spring (season), forming the simile.
segments.s059-g8: سراپای (from سراپا) means “throughout / entirely,” and پر governs the nouns that follow to mean “full (of).” بوی, رنگ and نگار are a coordinated list: “scent, color, and ornament/beauty,” with repeated و as the conjunction linking the items; the whole phrase describes being completely filled with fragrance, color, and ornament.
segments.s059-g41: سپیدی مویش (literally “the whiteness of her hair”) is a noun phrase: سپیدی (the whiteness; noun) + مویش (مو “hair” with enclitic -ش for “her/his”). بزیبد is an archaic/poetic present form meaning “is pleasing / befits / makes beautiful”; همی is the old continuous particle that marks an ongoing or habitual present and here follows the verb. Word order is noun phrase → verb → particle, i.e. “the whiteness of her hair was/wasfit(ting).”
segments.s059-g42: تو گویی که is a formulaic classical phrase meaning “one would say / it seems as if”; تو is the vocative/old second‑person form used idiomatically, گویی here is a verb form meaning “you say / one might say” (note: the provided noun senses for گفت‌و/گویی do not fit the verb usage, so that item is non‑matching). که introduces the subordinate clause. دلها is the grammatical subject (“hearts”); فریبد is the present poetic verb (3rd‑person form) meaning “wins/charms” (i.e. causes to be deceived/charmed); همی again marks the continuous/habitual aspect. Overall: “you might say that it charms hearts.”
segments.s059-g43: چو here is the temporal conjunction “when.” بشنید is a past tense verb “(she) heard.” The verb comes before the subject (typical of literary Persian), so the phrase reads “when Rudabeh heard that conversation.” آن گفت‌گوی (“that conversation”) is the object/phrase heard.
segments.s059-g44: برافروخت is a past intransitive/predicative verb meaning “she kindled / flushed (i.e. blushed).” Then و links a second clause: گلنارگون (an adjective “pomegranate‑coloured, rosy”) + کرد (past of کردن “to make/caused”) + روی (“face”). In this construction کرد is causative: “she made (her) face pomegranate‑red.” The verbs are past tense and describe successive actions.
segments.s059-g45: دلش is دل “heart” + enclitic -ش “her/his,” so “her heart.” گشت is the past of گشتن used as 'became.' پرآتش is a compound adjective “full of fire / passionate.” از introduces the cause (“because of / from”), مهر is “love/affection,” and زال is the proper name Zal. The whole clause literally: “Her heart became fiery because of (her) love for Zal.”
segments.s059-g46: از او دور شد is literal “from him became distant” = “she drew away from him” (از = from; او = him/her; دور = distant; شد = became). خورد here is not literal “eat” but the light/intransitive sense “befell / was overcome” (a common poetic usage meaning she was struck/overpowered). و links adjectives/states: آرام “calm/quiet” and هال “state/condition / motionless state” (a poetic noun). So: “she withdrew, was overcome, and lay quiet and motionless.”
segments.s059-g47: چو is the temporal conjunction “when.” بگرفت is past of گرفتن “took/seized.” جای خرد literally “the place of wisdom” (جای = place; خرد = wisdom/reason). آرزوی (wish/desire) is the agent that has taken that place. The clause reads: “When desire seized/took the place of wisdom.”
segments.s059-g48: دگر شد is a concise literary way of saying “became different / changed” (دگر = other/different; شد = became). The following prepositional phrases with به introduce the spheres in which change occurred: به رای “in opinion/counsel,” به آیین “in manner/custom,” و خوی “and (in) disposition.” The verbs and prepositions link the nouns as coordinates describing how she altered.
segments.s059-g33: رخش (Rokh) is the proper noun/name of the steed and comes first as the subject. پژمراننده is a present-participle/adjectival form from پژمردن (“to fade/wither”) used attributively to describe رخش (“the steed whose colour is fading”). ارغوان is a color noun (“crimson/purple”) modifying the participle; word order is noun + participle + color in this poetic inversion.
segments.s059-g34: جوان سال is a nominal phrase literally ‘young-year’ = ‘young in years/young-aged’ (جوان modifying سال); the copula (‘is/was’) is omitted as typical in poetry. و بیدار links an additional predicate ‘and alert/awake.’ و connects the next clause, and بختش is a possessed noun (بخت + suffix -ش “his”); جوان after it is a predicate adjective (‘his fortune is youthful’).
segments.s059-g35: به + noun phrase (به کین اندرون) gives a locative/inner relation (‘within/toward vengeance’). چون introduces a simile (‘like’). نهنگ is the simile’s image (‘whale’), and بلاست is the predicative nominal (بلا + -ست ‘is a calamity’): literally ‘within him, hatred is a calamity like a whale.’
segments.s059-g36: به زین اندرون is a locative phrase ‘within/on that saddle.’ تیز چنگ is a noun phrase ‘keen/sharp claw’ (تیز modifying چنگ). اژدهاست is the predicate attributing the claw to a dragon (literally ‘is of a dragon’); the verb/copula is fused in the poetic ending -ست. The whole clause reads ‘within the saddle is the dragon’s sharp claw.’
segments.s059-g37: نشاننده is a present-participle/agentive (‘marking/one who marks’). خاک در کین is an object/locative cluster ‘the earth in (the grip of) vengeance’ (در + کین forming a genitival/locative idea). به خون indicates the manner/result ‘with blood’ (i.e. marking the earth with blood). The participle construction leaves the agent implied (poetic ellipsis).
segments.s059-g38: فشاننده is another present-participle ‘sprinkling/scattering’ used attributively. خنجر is the noun (‘dagger’) which فشاننده modifies, and آبگون is a color/appearance adjective ‘bluish/pale’ following the noun. Grammatically this produces ‘the bluish dagger that scatters (blood).’
segments.s059-g39: از آهو literally ‘from/of the gazelle’ (از marks source or cause). همان کش uses همان ‘that same’ plus کش as an agentive/clausal element (کش here forms an agentive sense: ‘the one who…’ or ‘that which causes’). سپیدست موی is a poetic adjective phrase: سپیدست (literally ‘white‑handed’ but used here to mean ‘white‑haired’ or ‘making white’) with موی ‘hair’ (سپیدست موی = ‘whose hair is white’ or ‘which makes hair white’). The whole unit is an appositive describing the gazelle/lover.
segments.s059-g40: بگوید سخن is a verbal phrase ‘(they) speak/say words’ (بگوید is the present/indefinite 3rd‑person form used in narrative/poetic register). مردم عیب جوی is a noun phrase ‘people who seek faults’ (مردم = people; عیب‌جوی = fault‑seeking, formed from عیب + جوی ‘searcher’). Together: ‘people given to fault‑finding speak these words (about the gazelle).’
segments.s059-g9: شگفتی is the noun ‘a marvel/wonder’ serving as the sentential theme. The phrase به رودابه اندر بماند is a classical word order where the prepositional phrase (به رودابه اندر, ‘in/within Rudabeh’) precedes the verb بماند (‘remained’). بماند here is narrative past (“remained/was left”), so the whole segment reads literally “a marvel remained in Rudabeh.”
segments.s059-g10: همی is a habitual/iterative particle giving a past‑habit sense (“kept on/continually”). نام یزدان is the direct phrase ‘the name of God’. The first بر is the preposition ‘upon/over’, او is the pronoun ‘her’, and the second بر is part of the older compound verbal expression (compare برخواند) so that بر + بخواند functions as ‘utter/exclaim/call’. بخواند here is a past narrative form used with همی to express repeated past action: “people kept exclaiming the name of God over her.”
segments.s059-g11: یکی سرو دید is straightforward: یکی (a certain/one) + سرو (cypress, used metaphorically for a tall beautiful person) + دید (saw, past). از برش is a classical locative/relational phrase meaning ‘from/by her side’ (literally “from her‑side”), placed before the descriptive noun گرد ماه which links the image to the moon; overall the clause means “he saw a cypress at her side (with a moonlike aspect).”
segments.s059-g12: نهاده is the past participle/past narrative “placed/put.” ز عنبر is an ablative/material phrase “of/from amber” (here: an amber cap), and به سر بر کلاه is the prepositional target phrase ‘a cap on (its) head’ — note the older order: material (ز عنبر) then goal (به سر) then the object (کلاه). The implied agent (the moon) is understood from the preceding گرد ماه, so the line reads “the moon had placed, of amber, a cap upon the head.”
segments.s059-g13: به دیبا و گوهر bi‑constructed phrase: به marks the material/means (‘in/with’), دیبا ‘brocade’ and گوهر ‘gem/jewel’ are coordinated by و, and بیاراسته is a predicative participle meaning ‘adorned’ (classical spelling without a separating hamze). The phrase thus functions as a adjectival phrase, “adorned with brocade and jewels.”
segments.s059-g14: به سان is an idiom ‘like/as’; بهشتی (literally ‘heavenly’ or ‘paradise’) is used as the comparison’s noun, and پر از is the classical construction ‘full of’ followed by the genitive/compliment از + خواسته (‘desire/things desired’). Together: “like a paradise full of desires.”
segments.s059-g15: بپرسید is the past narrative form ‘asked’ (3rd‑person past). سیندخت is the agent/subject (Sindokht) and مهراب را is the definite direct object marked by را. Word order is subject after the verb, a common option in classical Persian narrative: ‘Sindokht asked Mehrab.’
segments.s059-g16: ز خوشاب is an ablative/instrumental phrase ‘with/from sweet water’ (خوشاب = pleasant or sweet water/drink). بگشاد is the past form of گشاد(ن) here meaning ‘opened/loosed/served’ (the provided dictionary senses treat گشاد as ‘wide’ and ‘opening’, so the verbal reading is the relevant one). عناب را is the definite object ‘the jujube(s)’ marked by را. So the clause reads roughly “(he) opened/served the jujubes with sweet water.”
segments.s059-g25: This is a poetic, slightly inverted prepositional phrase: به گیتی در = در گیتی ('in the world'), از پهلوانان = 'of/from the champions' (i.e. 'among the champions'), and گرد is an adverbial/poetic particle meaning 'around/about' or 'throughout'. Word order is emphatic and typical of epic Persian.
segments.s059-g26: پی زال = 'after Zāl' (پی used adverbially = 'after/in the wake of'). زر is the object 'gold'. نیارد here is an archaic/poetic negative form of آوردن ('bring') meaning 'does not/ did not bring' (so 'کس نیارد' = 'no one brought'); سپرد is a past/simple verb 'handed/entrusted' but here functions as the clause's final verbal idea — poetic sequence makes the precise agency slightly compressed. In short: 'after Zāl no one brought/gave gold.'
segments.s059-g27: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when'. دست و عنانش = 'hand and his reins' (عنَان + possessive -ش), where the conjunction و links two nouns. بر ایوان نگار = prepositional phrase 'on the beloved's iwan/porch' (بر + محل + نگار as genitive target). The phrase is a circumstantial time/place clause introducing the scene.
segments.s059-g28: نبینی is an impersonal/second-person-present negative 'you/one do(es) not see' (poetic usage to mean 'one does not see'). نه بر زین = 'not on the saddle' (negation + locative), چون او = 'such as he / like him' (comparative), and یک سوار = 'a single rider' (indefinite noun phrase placed for emphasis at the end). The clause states that no rider like him is seen on a saddle.
segments.s059-g29: This clause uses poetic juxtaposition instead of an explicit ezāfe: 'دل شیر نر' = 'a heart of (a) male lion'. دارد is the present tense 'has'. The conjunction و links the next nominal phrase 'زور پیل' = 'the strength of an elephant' where the verb 'has' is understood to govern both nouns (ellipsis of a repeated verb).
segments.s059-g30: دو دستش = 'his two hands' (دست + possessive suffix -ش, with numeral before the noun). به کردار = a comparative prepositional phrase 'in the manner of / like'. دریای نیل = 'the sea of the Nile' (genitive), used metaphorically to express vastness or power. Overall this is a simile: 'his two hands (are) like the Nile.'
segments.s059-g31: چو = 'when'. بر گاه = 'at the court/royal seat' (بر + گاه used as place). باشد ... بود is a poetic double copula: باشد is a subjunctive/stative auxiliary and بود is the past/copular verb; together they present a stative description ('he was / he is so'). در افشان is an adjectival phrase (افشان used poetically = 'having hair/robe spread' or 'resplendent'), so the whole clause says 'when at court he was resplendent/arrayed.'
segments.s059-g32: Parallel structure to the previous clause: چو در جنگ = 'when in war/battle'. باشد ... بود again gives a stative descriptive formula. سر افشان is a fixed poetic phrase meaning 'with head/hair thrown about' (i.e. head‑tossing, hair flying), portraying boldness in battle. The two clauses contrast courtly appearance with battlefield abandon.
segments.s059-g17: که introduces a subordinate temporal clause ('when/that') and each چون functions here as a temporal conjunction 'when/as'. رفتی and آمدی are perfect/past 2nd-person singular verbs ('you went', 'you came'); in classical Persian the verb appears at the end of the clause. امروز is a temporal adverb 'today'; و simply coordinates the two temporal clauses.
segments.s059-g18: که here introduces the main clause that expresses a wish/curse. کوتاه is an adjective 'short' and باد is the optative/exhortative particle 'may it be' (classical usage). از تو marks source/agent 'from you', and دست بدی is a nominal phrase 'the (evil/harmful) hand' (دست = hand/power, بدی = evil/harm). The word order places the adjective and optative before the affected NP.
segments.s059-g19: چه is an exclamatory 'what' introducing astonishment. مردست is an archaic/fused form (= مرد است) meaning 'is a man' (verb 'to be' fused to the noun). این پیر سر پور سام is a nominal phrase: این 'this', پیر 'old/venerable', سر here 'chief/head/leader', پور سام 'son of Sam' (patronymic).
segments.s059-g20: همی is an adverb meaning 'ever/still' (emphatic/continuous). تخت یاد آیدش‌گر is a clause where تخت 'throne' is the theme and یاد آیدش‌گر is a conditional/impersonal phrase ('if it/that comes to his memory' — آیدش‌گر functions as 'if it comes to him'). کنام ('near the bosom/side') is a locative complement. The conditional particle گر attaches to آیدش to mean 'if/when'.
segments.s059-g21: خوی مردمی is a noun phrase: خوی 'disposition/character' modified by مردمی 'of the people/people-pleasing'. هیچ is a negative quantifier 'no/none' (here 'not at all'), دارد is the present tense 'has', and همی is an adverb emphasizing habitual/continuing action ('continually/still'). Together they mean 'he has no popular disposition.'
segments.s059-g22: پی is a prepositional/adverbial 'after/in pursuit of' (here indicating following or relying on), نامداران is plural nominal 'the renowned/illustrious ones', سپارد is a present-tense verb 'he entrusts/commits' (سپردن), and همی again adds habitual meaning. The object entrusted is omitted and understood from context (i.e. he entrusts affairs/authority to famed men).
segments.s059-g23: چنین 'thus/so' modifies the whole speech-act; داد is the past tense 'gave'. مهراب is the speaker's name (proper name; not one of the listed noun senses), پاسخ 'answer' is the object, and بدوی is an epithet/adverb meaning 'original/at first' or 'in that primitive/straightforward way' qualifying the reply.
segments.s059-g24: که‌ای is a vocative formula (that + ای) introducing direct address ('O ...'). سرو سیمین is a compound epithet: سرو 'cypress' used metaphorically for a tall/elegant person and سیمین (implied here) 'silver/bright' qualifies it. بر is a preposition 'on/upon' used idiomatically in the phrase بر ماه روی 'with a moon-like face' where ماه 'moon' and روی 'face' form the simile 'moon-faced'. The whole is a direct apostrophe: 'O cypress, silver, moon-faced!'.

sentences.s059-s1: So it was that Mehrab, at the break of day, went off and came back from that palace.
sentences.s059-s2: He passed toward his bedchamber; he kept walking about his garden.
sentences.s059-s3: There were two suns in his iwan — Sindukht and Rudabeh, moon‑faced.
sentences.s059-s4: Arrayed like a spring garden, entirely full of scent, color, and ornament.
sentences.s059-s21: The whiteness of her hair made her lovely; one would say that it charmed hearts.
sentences.s059-s22: When Rudabeh heard that conversation, she blushed and made her face pomegranate‑red.
sentences.s059-s23: Her heart became aflame with love for Zal; she withdrew from him, was overcome, and lay quiet and motionless.
sentences.s059-s24: When desire took the place of wisdom, she changed; her opinions, manners, and disposition became different.
sentences.s059-s17: Rokh, its crimson fading, was young in years and alert, and his fortune was young.
sentences.s059-s18: Within him hatred is a calamity like a whale; within that saddle lies the dragon’s sharp claw.
sentences.s059-s19: A marker of the earth in vengeance, staining it with blood; a bluish dagger that scatters (it).
sentences.s059-s20: From the gazelle — that very one who makes the hair turn white — say the words of fault‑finding people.
sentences.s059-s5: A marvel remained within Rūdābeh; people kept invoking the name of God over her.
sentences.s059-s6: He saw a single cypress at her side; the moon had placed an amber cap upon its head.
sentences.s059-s7: Adorned in brocade and jewels, like a paradise full of desires.
sentences.s059-s8: Sindokht asked Mehrāb; (then) he, with sweet water, opened/served the jujubes.
sentences.s059-s13: In the world, among the champions, after Zāl no one brought/gave gold.
sentences.s059-s14: When his hand and reins are on the beloved's iwan, you do not see upon a saddle a rider like him.
sentences.s059-s15: His heart is that of a male lion and his strength that of an elephant; his two hands are like the sea of the Nile.
sentences.s059-s16: When he is at the court he is splendid/arrayed; when he is in battle he goes with his head thrown back (his hair flying).
sentences.s059-s9: When you went today and when you came, may the hand of evil be shortened toward you.
sentences.s059-s10: What a man is this venerable elder, son of Sam; the throne ever comes to his mind when he is near the royal bosom.
sentences.s059-s11: He has no popular (pleasing-to-the-people) disposition at all; he continually entrusts [affairs] to the renowned.
sentences.s059-s12: Thus Mahrab gave this answer, saying, 'O silver-cypress with a face like the moon!'
